title: Nvidia's Rubin Chip Arrives in Late 2026. Is Now the Time to Buy This Artificial Intelligence (AI) Stock? - The Motley Fool
content: Nvidia is ready to launch its next-generation chip platform, but there are other factors to consider before you buy the stock. Nvidia (NVDA +5.56%) is the poster child of the artificial intelligence (AI) industry. Its high-powered chips are the brains that power AI. The company's leading position in the chip industry isn't lost on Wall Street, with the stock up 50% over the past year and over 18,000% over the past decade. That said, Nvidia's stock is down nearly 20% from its 52-week high, and it has a new chip platform on the way in late 2026. Is now the time to buy this stock, or should investors just keep it on their wish list? Nvidia's stock has fallen sharply in recent months. The AI stock's price drop puts it firmly in correction territory and on the verge of falling into its own personal bear market. The stock looks cheap relative to its own history. For example, Nvidia's five-year average price-to-earnings ratio is around 64x, but its current P/E is only 34x. Its five-year average price-to-book ratio is 30x, but its current P/B ratio is just under 26x. If you are a long-term believer in AI's ability to change the world, this may appear like an opportunity to add a leading AI company to your portfolio at a reasonable price. There's only one problem: the stock still looks expensive on an absolute level. For example, the average technology stock has a P/E ratio of around 34x and an average P/B ratio of 8.5x. The S&P 500 index (^GSPC +2.91%) has an average P/E of roughly 28x and a P/B ratio of 5x. If you have a value bias, you'll probably find that Nvidia is still too expensive to buy. Beyond valuation, there's another issue that needs careful consideration: rising oil and natural gas prices. The geopolitical conflict in the Middle East has driven energy prices sharply higher. Higher energy prices reverberate through the economy; they don't just impact the price of gasoline. For example, natural gas is a key fuel for many electric utilities. That will likely lead to higher electricity costs, as rising natural gas prices are passed on to customers. AI uses a significant amount of electricity, so higher energy prices could make AI more expensive to use. Also, building out AI infrastructure is the next big leg in AI development. Oil and natural gas are used throughout the construction and materials sectors. Higher energy costs will make it more expensive to build data centers and more costly to expand the electricity grid to power those data centers. Those are just a few examples of how AI could be impacted. Higher energy prices will also make basic necessities like food and clothing more expensive. There were already recession fears, with consumers trading down to lower-priced stores to tighten their budgets. If higher energy prices lead to a broader economic pullback, it wouldn't be surprising to see major capital investment projects, such as AI infrastructure spending, put on hold as well. Some projects could even be canceled. Given the market backdrop, it wouldn't be shocking to see the AI bubble that pushed Nvidia and the broader market higher for years continue to deflate. Given that Wall Street has a bad habit of going too far in both the positive and negative directions, Nvidia's current decline could easily keep going. Unless you believe very strongly in the future of AI, you should probably watch Nvidia from the sidelines. Notably, it took a quarter of a century for dot-com darling Cisco (CSCO +0.73%) to work its way back from its deep drawdown after the dot-com bubble burst at the turn of the century. If the AI bubble bursts, you'll likely have plenty of time to buy Nvidia stock and possibly at even more attractive prices. Reuben Gregg Brewer has no position in any of the stocks mentioned. title: Could Super Micro Computer's Troubles Sink Nvidia's Stock? - The Motley Fool
content: Multiple people connected with Super Micro, including its co-founder, have allegedly been smuggling Nvidia's chips to China. The U.S. government has charged multiple people connected with Super Micro Computer (SMCI +7.91%) with smuggling Nvidia (NVDA +5.56%)'s advanced chips to China, including Wally Liaw, a co-founder of Super Micro. Given the close relationship between the two companies, it's not surprising that Nvidia's stock has been falling amid these concerning developments. While there's no reason to believe the tech company is involved in any wrongdoing, it would suggest that at least some of its growth can be attributed to the Chinese market -- something that investors have largely assumed hasn't been the case. Here's why this can be a big problem for the tech giant. Investors have been bullish on Nvidia's stock due to the expectation that there is much more growth to come from the business in the future. And one market that has been largely off-limits of late but that has been seen as a potentially lucrative growth opportunity for Nvidia is China. Last year, CEO Jensen Huang estimated that the overall artificial intelligence (AI) market in China could reach $50 billion within the coming years. For Nvidia, China has represented a huge growth opportunity, and that has enabled investors to remain fairly bullish about its long-term prospects, given that it's doing so well without a huge market to tap into just yet. But if people connected with Super Micro have truly been diverting billions of dollars' worth of chips to China, then Nvidia's growth would effectively factor in a piece of the Chinese market, even if the alleged figure is relatively modest at around $2.5 billion. The bigger risk, however, may be that the U.S. government puts in more restrictive measures on the Chinese market in light of this scandal. title: Nvidia (NVDA) Shares Slide 2.2% as Risk-Off Sentiment Grips the $4.4 Trillion AI Leader - FinancialContent
content: SANTA CLARA, CA — Shares of Nvidia (NASDAQ: NVDA) fell 2.2% on Monday, March 30, 2026, as a wave of "risk-off" sentiment swept through the technology sector. Despite its status as the world’s most valuable company with a staggering $4.4 trillion market capitalization, the AI juggernaut could not escape a broader market rotation that saw investors pull back from high-flying growth stocks. The decline, while modest in percentage terms, wiped nearly $100 billion in market value from the chipmaker in a single trading session, underscoring the immense weight Nvidia now carries in global indexes. The sell-off appears to be driven by a combination of end-of-quarter profit-taking and mounting anxiety regarding the long-term return on investment (ROI) for artificial intelligence infrastructure. As Nvidia remains the primary beneficiary of the AI build-out, it has become the de facto barometer for the health of the entire tech ecosystem. Today’s dip marks a rare moment of turbulence for a stock that has redefined market leadership over the past three years, leaving investors to wonder if the "AI premium" is finally meeting its ceiling. The 2.2% decline on March 30 follows a period of intense volatility and high expectations. Just two weeks ago, Nvidia hosted its annual GPU Technology Conference (GTC), where CEO Jensen Huang unveiled the much-anticipated "Rubin" architecture. The announcement of the R100 GPU and the Vera CPU—built on Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company’s (NYSE: TSM) 3nm process—initially sent shares to all-time highs as the company promised another 3x leap in AI compute efficiency. However, as the initial euphoria of the product launch faded, the reality of a "higher-for-longer" interest rate environment began to weigh on the NASDAQ. The timeline leading to today’s drop began early in the morning when the 10-year Treasury yield ticked up to 4.6%, a level that historically triggers a migration of capital away from high-multiple tech stocks. By mid-day, reports from several tier-one cloud service providers suggested a "normalization" of capital expenditure budgets for the second half of 2026. While demand for Nvidia’s Blackwell (GB200) chips remains robust, the market reacted nervously to the hint that the triple-digit growth rates of 2024 and 2025 might be transitioning into a more moderate, albeit still significant, growth phase. Key institutional players, including major pension funds and index trackers, appeared to be rebalancing portfolios ahead of the Q1 close, contributing to the downward pressure on Nvidia’s shares. While Nvidia bore the brunt of the day's headlines, the ripple effects were felt across the semiconductor and cloud landscapes. Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ: AMD) saw its shares decline by 1.8%, as it continues to battle Nvidia for the number two spot in the AI accelerator market with its MI350 series. Intel (NASDAQ: INTC) fared slightly better, dropping only 0.5%, as investors look toward its foundry business as a potential hedge against Nvidia’s hardware dominance. The "losers" of the day were primarily the high-beta AI software startups that rely on Nvidia’s ecosystem; many of these smaller firms saw their valuations contract by 4% or more as the cost of capital remains stubbornly high. On the winning side of the ledger, value-oriented defensive sectors—such as consumer staples and utilities—saw modest inflows as the "risk-off" sentiment took hold. Some analysts argue that the "hyperscalers" like Microsoft (NASDAQ: MSFT) and Alphabet (NASDAQ: GOOGL) may actually benefit in the long term from a cooling of hardware prices. If Nvidia’s pricing power experiences even a slight softening due to increased competition or custom silicon efforts like Google's TPU v7, the margins for these cloud giants could see a significant boost. Furthermore, specialized cooling and power management companies, which are essential for the massive Rubin-based data centers, remained relatively resilient, suggesting that the underlying infrastructure build-out is far from over. Today's market action reflects a broader industrial trend: the transition from the "Build" phase to the "Deployment" phase of the AI revolution. In 2024 and 2025, the market rewarded companies that could supply the picks and shovels—principally Nvidia. By early 2026, the narrative has shifted toward the "sovereign AI" movement and the emergence of "Agentic AI." Governments in Europe and the Middle East are now massive customers, but they are also more price-sensitive and regulatory-conscious than the initial wave of Silicon Valley early adopters. The 2.2% drop also highlights the regulatory scrutiny facing the industry. With Nvidia’s market cap exceeding $4 trillion, it has become a focal point for global antitrust regulators who are concerned about the company’s vertically integrated stack, from the NVLink interconnects to the CUDA software layer. While no specific regulatory action was announced today, the shadow of potential intervention remains a persistent headwind. Historically, companies that reach this level of market dominance—such as Cisco in 2000 or Microsoft in the late 1990s—eventually face a period of "valuation digestion" where the stock price takes a breather even as the underlying business continues to perform well. Looking forward, the short-term outlook for Nvidia will likely depend on the Q1 earnings report scheduled for May. Investors will be laser-focused on the company’s guidance for the Rubin architecture rollout in the second half of the year. If Nvidia can prove that the upgrade cycle from Blackwell to Rubin is accelerating, the current 2.2% dip may be viewed as a classic "buying opportunity." However, a strategic pivot may be required if the hyperscalers continue to lean more heavily on their own custom ASICs. Market participants should expect continued volatility as Nvidia navigates its new role as the world’s most influential stock. The challenge for Nvidia in the coming months will be to maintain its industry-leading margins while fending off an increasingly sophisticated group of competitors. Potential scenarios include a period of horizontal trading as the market waits for the next "killer app" in AI to drive the next wave of hardware demand. Investors should watch for updates on TSMC’s 3nm yields and any shifts in the Federal Reserve’s interest rate trajectory, as these will be the primary drivers of Nvidia’s valuation in the mid-term. In summary, the 2.2% decline in Nvidia shares on March 30, 2026, is less a sign of fundamental weakness and more a symptom of a maturing market. At a $4.4 trillion valuation, Nvidia is no longer just a chip company; it is the cornerstone of the modern digital economy. The "risk-off" sentiment seen today is a reminder that even the most dominant leaders are susceptible to macroeconomic gravity and the pressures of end-of-quarter rebalancing. Moving forward, the market is likely to remain sensitive to any news regarding AI ROI and hardware spending cycles. For investors, the key takeaways are to remain vigilant regarding sector rotation and to watch the "Rubin" production timeline closely. While the long-term significance of Nvidia’s role in the AI era remains undisputed, the days of easy, straight-line gains may be giving way to a more complex and volatile trading environment. title: Beyond the NVIDIA ‘Sugar High’: Why I’d Pivot My Portfolio Toward the ‘Boring’ Side of AI - 24/7 Wall St.
content: Nvidia (NVDA) trades below its $170 support level with a 34.1 P/E ratio, facing risks from competitors developing silicon as hyperscalers build their own chips. Nvidia’s $4 trillion market cap and 50% yearly gain may mask uncertainty about whether the company can defend its chip dominance as major tech firms invest heavily in custom silicon. Are you ahead, or behind on retirement? SmartAsset's free tool can match you with a financial advisor in minutes to help you answer that today. Each advisor has been carefully vetted, and must act in your best interests. Don't waste another minute; learn more here.(Sponsor) Even long-time market darling Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA | NVDA Price Prediction) is starting to look a bit toppy, especially after plunging to the lower end of its lengthy consolidation channel. Undoubtedly, if Nvidia breaks through the low end, it’ll be tough to tell what the next stop will be, and it’s perhaps the technical breakdown that could cause a panic as investors rush to the exits while the profits are still intact. Of course, the recent slide has been quite terrifying for latecomers who bought the sideways move. That said, the GPU titan is still up 50% in the past year and commands that $4 trillion market cap (at least as of the time of this writing). As Michael Burry starts to look right in a big way, questions linger as to what investors who still believe in the future of the AI trade should do. Undoubtedly, my biggest concern with Nvidia is what the endgame will be once the hyperscalers make their own custom silicon. This is a major question mark that I’ve brought up in a number of prior pieces. Sure, GPUs aren’t going anywhere, and the Nvidia ecosystem is sticky enough to make competing with it far less economical, at least on the surface. But I think it could prove tough as the rest of the Magnificent Seven looks to eat their own cooking, so to speak, while potentially teaming up with each other to meet their AI computing needs. Of course, Nvidia will continue to be the place to get chips as the AI revolution moves on and Vera Rubin demand looks to blow away the numbers again. Either way, Nvidia stock might still be considered a tad on the expensive side, especially for Mag Seven standards, at 34.1 times trailing price-to-earnings (P/E), especially considering the cyclicality and downside risks of chips. Depending on your perspective, Nvidia stock is either too cheap (given its growth) or too expensive (given the potential for growth and margins to move lower in the coming years). Either way, I think it all comes down to whether you think Nvidia can continue defending its moat against competition as more firms look to get in the custom silicon game. Are you ahead, or behind on retirement? SmartAsset's free tool can match you with a financial advisor in minutes to help you answer that today. Each advisor has been carefully vetted, and must act in your best interests. Don't waste another minute; learn more here.(Sponsor) There are massive rewards to be had in the AI chip scene, and that alone is enough for firms to spend substantial cash on the effort, even if it means only gaining a slice of the pie. With Nvidia trading below its strong support level ($170 per share), I have no idea what the next major move will be. Technically speaking, it’s a tough name to consider doubling down on. Personally, I have difficulty valuing the stock, given the uncertainties that cloud the future of the AI trade. Will AI demand stay off the charts for more years? If so, how many? And if the AI boom lasts longer, will Nvidia continue to be the king? Or will credible challengers arise? I don’t have the answers, but I think “boring” might be a place to bet on AI with somewhat less risk. Look no further than cooling systems, energy plays, and AI-driven software (think the “disrupted” SaaS stocks that are already down so much). Whether we’re talking about Vertiv (NASDAQ:VRT) and its role in chilling the scorching-hot AI chips, the energy producers that will actually keep the data centers running, or the enterprise software companies (Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) is the most obvious play) that are actually taking steps to allow customers to effectively automate workflows, I do think that there are other places to be, apart from Nvidia, as the AI trade encounters its first big stumble. Find out why NVIDIA's 52.8% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers. A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and then discounting those back to today using a required rate of return. It focuses on cash that could be available to shareholders rather than on reported earnings. For NVIDIA, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $97.2b. Analyst and extrapolated projections suggest free cash flow could reach about $410.2b in 2031, with a series of annual forecasts in between that are discounted back to arrive at a present value. Putting those projected cash flows together, the DCF model produces an estimated intrinsic value of about $188.20 per share. Compared to the recent share price of US$167.52, the model implies the stock is about 11.0% undervalued based purely on these cash flow assumptions. Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests NVIDIA is undervalued by 11.0%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 62 more high quality undervalued stocks. Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for NVIDIA. For a profitable company that is generating meaningful earnings, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to see how much you are paying for each dollar of profit. It captures what the market is currently willing to pay in light of the company’s growth outlook and perceived risk. Higher growth expectations and lower risk usually support a higher “normal” P/E, while slower expected growth or higher risk typically point to a lower one. NVIDIA currently trades on a P/E of 33.90x. That sits below the semiconductor industry average of about 38.17x and well below the peer group average of 85.49x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio is an attempt to refine this comparison. It estimates what a more suitable P/E could be, given factors like NVIDIA’s earnings growth profile, profit margins, industry, market cap and risk characteristics, rather than relying only on broad peer or industry averages. For NVIDIA, this Fair Ratio is 56.96x, which is materially higher than the current 33.90x. This gap suggests the current market multiple is below what the Fair Ratio implies for NVIDIA. This is where Narratives come in, giving you a simple way to connect your view of NVIDIA’s business to a set of numbers and a fair value that you can actually work with. A Narrative is your story about a company, written in financial terms, where you spell out what you believe about future revenue, earnings and margins, and the model turns that into an estimated fair value per share. On Simply Wall St’s Community page, Narratives are an easy tool to use, already set up for millions of investors so you can pick a view that matches your thinking rather than building a spreadsheet from scratch. Each Narrative ties the story to a forecast and then to a fair value, so you can compare that fair value to NVIDIA’s current share price and decide whether it currently looks expensive or cheap relative to that story. Because Narratives update when new earnings, news or valuation inputs are added, you are not stuck with a static snapshot. Your chosen view on NVIDIA keeps moving as the facts change. For example, one detailed NVIDIA Narrative on Simply Wall St assumes revenue of about US$570b by 2031 and applies a 45x P/E to arrive at a fair value that implies strong upside, while a more cautious Narrative assumes far lower earnings growth and a future P/E closer to 18x, leading to a fair value that sits below today’s US$167.52 share price. This is not about picking the “right” forecast. It is about choosing the version of the future that feels closest to your own view and checking whether today’s price lines up with that story or not. title: Prediction: This Will Be Nvidia's Stock Price 3 Years From Now - The Motley Fool
content: Nvidia (NVDA +5.56%) shares are up by around 525% over the past three years, making it a top performer over that time frame. While I doubt another 525% run-up is in the cards for the next three years, I do think the stock could be headed significantly higher. Nvidia's business success is tied to the AI buildout: The longer it lasts, the better the stock will do. It will also benefit from increased AI spending, although many hyperscalers are spending all of their cash flows on building new data centers already. However, one thing investors need to keep in mind is the mix of spending. Right now, many of the data centers announced last year are under construction, so a greater percentage of capital expenditures is going to the physical construction and infrastructure costs versus chip and hardware costs. This suggests there's room for further growth in Nvidia's sales over the next few years, and its projections back that up. During Nvidia's GTC 2026 conference this month, CEO Jensen Huang predicted that cumulative sales of Blackwell and Rubin chips will reach $1 trillion by the end of 2027. During last year's conference, he projected that 2025 and 2026 sales would be $500 billion, so that suggests Nvidia will clear at least $500 billion in AI chip sales during 2027. Wall Street analysts currently project $480 billion in sales, which sticks with a common theme of the analyst community under-projecting Nvidia's growth rates. But that's just year two of the projection. What might year three look like? It's hard to say, but based on Nvidia's guidance and several third-party projections, the pace of the AI buildout is expected to accelerate through 2030. From the end of this fiscal year to the end of next fiscal year, Wall Street analysts expect Nvidia to add another $110 billion in sales. If we add another $110 billion to the $480 billion Wall Street projects, that would give Nvidia $590 billion in total revenue. And let's round that up to $600 billion, since we expect that Wall Street is already guiding a bit light for next year. Our experts' top-rated cash back card just got even better.
